passage: Seismologists once assumed that the distribution of earthquakes across Earth's crust was largely unpredictable. Early field surveys seemed to support this view, [. Although more systematic analysis later revealed] that the vast majority of seismic events cluster along well-defined fault lines and tectonic plate boundaries. This discovery transformed earthquake risk assessment and led directly to the modern practice of probabilistic seismic hazard analysis.

passageMetadata.source: Original composition based on geophysics and seismology

stem: Which choice best maintains sentence boundaries?

choices.label: A
choices.text: . Although more systematic analysis later revealed

choices.label: B
choices.text: , although more systematic analysis later revealed

choices.label: C
choices.text: ; although more systematic analysis later revealed

choices.label: D
choices.text: : although more systematic analysis later revealed

explanation: The underlined portion connects two parts of the same sentence. The LEFT side, 'Early field surveys seemed to support this view,' is a complete independent clause. The RIGHT side, 'more systematic analysis later revealed that the vast majority of seismic events cluster along well-defined fault lines and tectonic plate boundaries,' is an adverbial clause introduced by the subordinating conjunction 'although,' making the entire right-side clause dependent. Choice B correctly uses a comma to attach the dependent adverbial clause to the independent clause. Choice A creates an illegal fragment by placing a period before the 'Although' clause, leaving 'Although more systematic analysis later revealed...' as a dependent clause with no main clause of its own. Choice C incorrectly uses a semicolon before a dependent clause; semicolons must join two independent clauses. Choice D incorrectly uses a colon before a dependent clause; colons introduce elaborating words, phrases, or clauses but cannot precede a clause introduced by a subordinating conjunction that makes it dependent.

distractorRationale.A: NO CHANGE — Places a period before the subordinating conjunction 'Although,' creating a dependent-clause fragment: 'Although more systematic analysis later revealed that...' has no main clause and cannot stand alone as a sentence.
distractorRationale.B: CORRECT — A comma properly attaches the dependent adverbial clause ('although more systematic analysis later revealed...') to the preceding independent clause, following Standard English conventions for subordinating conjunctions.
distractorRationale.C: Semicolon before a dependent clause — Students who know semicolons join independent clauses may incorrectly apply one here, not recognizing that 'although' transforms the right side into a dependent clause that cannot follow a semicolon.
distractorRationale.D: Colon before a dependent clause — Students may mistake the colon for a flexible connector, but a colon must follow a complete independent clause and can only introduce a word, phrase, or independent clause as elaboration — not a dependent clause introduced by a subordinating conjunction.
